topic: A neighbor's tree falls on their property
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun, usually a warm blanket, felt sharp and accusatory as I watched the old oak shudder. Its massive limbs, thick as my torso, swayed with a slow, agonizing groan. My breath hitched in my throat, a sudden, cold clench spreading through my chest. Every instinct screamed at me to run, but my feet were rooted to the porch, watching the trajectory of the falling wood. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to will the noise, the impending impact, away from my senses. A small, involuntary whimper escaped my lips, swallowed instantly by the wind. I just stood there, a statue carved from sheer paralysis, the sound of splintering wood growing louder, a terrifying, undeniable reality closing in.

[story 2]

I kept glancing toward the edge of the yard, my gaze darting from the canopy to the fence line, a frantic, shallow sweep. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the railing of the deck until my knuckles turned white. The silence between the creaking branches was deafening, amplifying the frantic thumping of my own heart against my ribs. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the world tilt precariously. I wanted to scream, to demand that the giant tree just *stop*, but the air caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, rasping sound. I could only stare, my jaw slack, waiting for the inevitable crash that felt less like an event and more like a personal judgment.

[story 3]

"Oh, God," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy, barely audible over the rustling leaves. I backed away slowly, taking measured steps backward, my eyes locked on the trunk as it began its descent. My skin felt clammy, prickling with a sudden, intense awareness of every small sound—the distant traffic, the drip of dew from the eaves. I wrapped my arms tightly around myself, pulling them close to my chest as if a physical barrier could stop the sheer weight of the moment. My gaze dropped to the ground, unable to meet the towering threat, focusing instead on the small, cracked paving stones beneath my worn shoes, trying desperately to anchor myself to something solid.
